question:
Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician
keeps track of the number, $S(N)$, of successful free throws she has made
in her first $N$ attempts of the season.
Early in the season, $S(N)$  was less than 80\% of  $N$,
but by the end of the season, $S(N)$ was more than 80\% of $N$.
Was there necessarily a moment in between when $S(N)$ was exactly 80\% of
$N$?
solution:
Yes. Suppose otherwise. Then there would be an $N$ such that $S(N) < .8N$
and $S(N+1) > .8(N+1)$; that is, O'Keal's free throw percentage is under $80\%$
at some point, and after one subsequent free throw (necessarily made),
her percentage is over $80\%$. If she makes $m$ of her first $N$ free
throws, then $m/N < 4/5$ and $(m+1)/(N+1) > 4/5$. This means that $5m <
4n < 5m+1$, which is impossible since then $4n$ is an integer between the
consecutive integers $5m$ and $5m+1$.

\textbf{Remark:}
This same argument works for any fraction of the form
$(n-1)/n$ for some integer $n>1$, but not for any other real number
between $0$ and $1$.

descriptive_long.question:
Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician
keeps track of the number, $successcount(attempttotal)$, of successful free throws she has made
in her first $attempttotal$ attempts of the season.
Early in the season, $successcount(attempttotal)$ was less than 80\% of $attempttotal$,
but by the end of the season, $successcount(attempttotal)$ was more than 80\% of $attempttotal$.
Was there necessarily a moment in between when $successcount(attempttotal)$ was exactly 80\% of
$attempttotal$?
descriptive_long.solution:
Yes. Suppose otherwise. Then there would be an $attempttotal$ such that $successcount(attempttotal) < .8\,attempttotal$
and $successcount(attempttotal+1) > .8\,(attempttotal+1)$; that is, O'Keal's free throw percentage is under $80\%$
at some point, and after one subsequent free throw (necessarily made),
her percentage is over $80\%$. If she makes $madecount$ of her first $attempttotal$ free
throws, then $madecount/attempttotal < 4/5$ and $(madecount+1)/(attempttotal+1) > 4/5$. This means that $5\,madecount <
4\,integerval < 5\,madecount+1$, which is impossible since then $4\,integerval$ is an integer between the
consecutive integers $5\,madecount$ and $5\,madecount+1$.

\textbf{Remark:}
This same argument works for any fraction of the form
$(integerval-1)/integerval$ for some integer $integerval>1$, but not for any other real number
between $0$ and $1$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician
keeps track of the number, $peppermill(marshmallow)$, of successful free throws she has made
in her first $marshmallow$ attempts of the season.
Early in the season, $peppermill(marshmallow)$  was less than 80\% of  $marshmallow$,
but by the end of the season, $peppermill(marshmallow)$ was more than 80\% of $marshmallow$.
Was there necessarily a moment in between when $peppermill(marshmallow)$ was exactly 80\% of
$marshmallow$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Yes. Suppose otherwise. Then there would be an $marshmallow$ such that $peppermill(marshmallow) < .8marshmallow$
and $peppermill(marshmallow+1) > .8(marshmallow+1)$; that is, O'Keal's free throw percentage is under $80\%$
at some point, and after one subsequent free throw (necessarily made),
her percentage is over $80\%$. If she makes $toadstool$ of her first $marshmallow$ free
throws, then $toadstool/marshmallow < 4/5$ and $(toadstool+1)/(marshmallow+1) > 4/5$. This means that $5toadstool <
4buttercup < 5toadstool+1$, which is impossible since then $4buttercup$ is an integer between the
consecutive integers $5toadstool$ and $5toadstool+1$.

\textbf{Remark:}
This same argument works for any fraction of the form
$(buttercup-1)/buttercup$ for some integer $buttercup>1$, but not for any other real number
between $0$ and $1$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician
keeps track of the number, $failureshots(unusedshots)$, of successful free throws she has made
in her first $unusedshots$ attempts of the season.
Early in the season, $failureshots(unusedshots)$  was less than 80\% of  $unusedshots$,
but by the end of the season, $failureshots(unusedshots)$ was more than 80\% of $unusedshots$.
Was there necessarily a moment in between when $failureshots(unusedshots)$ was exactly 80\% of
$unusedshots$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Yes. Suppose otherwise. Then there would be an $unusedshots$ such that $failureshots(unusedshots) < .8unusedshots$
and $failureshots(unusedshots+1) > .8(unusedshots+1)$; that is, O'Keal's free throw percentage is under $80\%$
at some point, and after one subsequent free throw (necessarily made),
her percentage is over $80\%$. If she makes $misscount$ of her first $unusedshots$ free
throws, then $misscount/unusedshots < 4/5$ and $(misscount+1)/(unusedshots+1) > 4/5$. This means that $5misscount <
4tinyintgr < 5misscount+1$, which is impossible since then $4tinyintgr$ is an integer between the
consecutive integers $5misscount$ and $5misscount+1$.

\textbf{Remark:}
This same argument works for any fraction of the form
$(tinyintgr-1)/tinyintgr$ for some integer $tinyintgr>1$, but not for any other real number
between $0$ and $1$.

garbled_string.question:
Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician
keeps track of the number, $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$, of successful free throws she has made
in her first $hjgrksla$ attempts of the season.
Early in the season, $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$  was less than 80\% of  $hjgrksla$,
but by the end of the season, $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$ was more than 80\% of $hjgrksla$.
Was there necessarily a moment in between when $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$ was exactly 80\% of
$hjgrksla$?
garbled_string.solution:
Yes. Suppose otherwise. Then there would be an $hjgrksla$ such that $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) < .8hjgrksla$
and $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla+1) > .8(hjgrksla+1)$; that is, O'Keal's free throw percentage is under $80\%$
at some point, and after one subsequent free throw (necessarily made),
her percentage is over $80\%$. If she makes $vcfqlsne$ of her first $hjgrksla$ free
throws, then $vcfqlsne/hjgrksla < 4/5$ and $(vcfqlsne+1)/(hjgrksla+1) > 4/5$. This means that $5vcfqlsne <
4pdkrmhzo < 5vcfqlsne+1$, which is impossible since then $4pdkrmhzo$ is an integer between the
consecutive integers $5vcfqlsne$ and $5vcfqlsne+1$.

\textbf{Remark:}
This same argument works for any fraction of the form
$(pdkrmhzo-1)/pdkrmhzo$ for some integer $pdkrmhzo>1$, but not for any other real number
between $0$ and $1$.

kernel_variant.question:
During a week-long practice session an archer named Asha fires arrows one at a time and keeps a running tally.  For every positive integer $N$ let $B(N)$ denote the number of bull-eyes she has hit in her first $N$ shots.  Early in the week her cumulative accuracy satisfied
\[
  B(N)<\tfrac{6}{7}N,\qquad\text{while by the end of the week it satisfied }\qquad B(N)>\tfrac{6}{7}N.
\]
Prove that there must have been some positive integer $N$ for which
\[
   B(N)=\tfrac{6}{7}N.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Assume, for the sake of contradiction, that no such N exists; i.e.
B(N)\neq 6/7\cdot N for every N.  Because the fraction B(N)/N starts below 6/7 and eventually exceeds 6/7, there is a first index N at which the cumulative accuracy jumps from below 6/7 to above 6/7.  Concretely, choose the smallest positive integer N such that
    B(N)/N < 6/7   and   B(N+1)/(N+1) > 6/7.
(The second inequality forces the (N+1)-st shot to be a bull's-eye.)  Write B(N)=m.  The two displayed inequalities translate to
    m/N < 6/7   \Rightarrow    7m < 6N,
    (m+1)/(N+1) > 6/7   \Rightarrow    7(m+1) > 6(N+1).
Expanding the second gives 7m+7 > 6N+6, i.e.
    6N < 7m+1.
Combining 7m < 6N < 7m+1 shows 6N is an integer strictly between the consecutive integers 7m and 7m+1, which is impossible.  This contradiction shows our original assumption was false, so there must indeed exist an integer N with B(N)=6/7\cdot N.

(The identical argument works verbatim for any target accuracy of the form (n-1)/n with n>1.)

core_steps: Assume there is never an attempt number with exactly the target success ratio.
Identify the first index where the cumulative ratio jumps from below the target to above it (from N to N+1).
Translate those two cumulative ratios into strict inequalities involving integers (made shots m and attempts N).
Show these inequalities force an integer to lie strictly between two consecutive integers, an impossibility.
Conclude the assumption is false, so an exact-ratio moment must exist.

slot1.description: Target success fraction; may be any (n−1)/n with integer n>1.

slot2.original: Basketball free throws by Shanille O'Keal
